title: Adjectives, Locations, and Existence
learningGoal: The learner will master the two classes of Japanese adjectives (i-adj and na-adj), negative adjective conjugations, the existence verbs (arimasu/imasu), and positional nouns (ue, shita, naka).
instructions: Welcome to Month 2! The pace and density of information increases significantly here. This mega-lesson covers how to describe objects, state what exists, and specify exactly where things are located in space. Take notes, especially on the difference between i-adjectives and na-adjectives.
conceptTitle: Describing the World and its Contents

lessonContent.bodyText: Japanese has two distinct types of adjectives that conjugate completely differently: 'i-adjectives' and 'na-adjectives'. An 'i-adjective' always ends in the Hiragana 'i' (い). 'na-adjectives' do not (with a few tricky exceptions).

targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Beautiful / Clean (EXCEPTION: Ends in 'i' but is a na-adjective!)

lessonContent.bodyText: To make a na-adjective negative, you just change 'desu' to 'ja arimasen' (just like nouns). BUT for i-adjectives, you must drop the final 'i' and add 'ku arimasen'.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: この ほんは たかいです。
この ほんは たかくありません。
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: This book is NOT expensive. (Drop 'i', add 'ku arimasen')

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: この としょかんは しずかです。
この としょかんは しずかじゃありません。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Kono toshokan wa shizuka ja arimasen.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: This library is NOT quiet. (Change desu to ja arimasen)

lessonContent.bodyText: When putting an adjective directly in front of a noun, i-adjectives stay the same. Na-adjectives require you to add the syllable 'na' (な) before the noun. This is why they are called na-adjectives!

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: しずかな としょかん

targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: A beautiful person (na-adj exception + na + noun)

lessonContent.bodyText: Japanese has two verbs for 'there is / there are'. 'Arimasu' (あります) is used for inanimate, non-moving objects (books, cars, trees). 'Imasu' (います) is used for animate, living things that move on their own (people, animals). We use the 'ga' (が) particle to mark what exists.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: くるまが あります。

lessonContent.bodyText: To say exactly WHERE something exists, we use positional nouns: Ue (top/above), Shita (bottom/under), Naka (inside), Mae (front), Ushiro (behind), and Tonari (next to). The structure is: [Place] no [Position] ni [Object] ga arimasu/imasu.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: つくえの うえに ほんが あります。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Tsukue no ue ni hon ga arimasu.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: On top of the desk, there is a book.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: はこの なかに ねこが います。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Hako no naka ni neko ga imasu.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Inside the box, there is a cat.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: きの したに いぬが いません。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Ki no shita ni inu ga imasen.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Under the tree, there is no dog.

learningObjective: The learner will fluidly conjugate i-adjectives and na-adjectives in both affirmative and negative forms, differentiate between Arimasu and Imasu, and construct complex sentences identifying the spatial location of objects.

examples.targetText: あの おおきい きの したに、きれいな ねこが います。
examples.transliteration: Ano ookii ki no shita ni, kirei na neko ga imasu.
examples.baseTranslation: Under that big tree over there, there is a beautiful cat.
examples.explanation: Mega-sentence combining a demonstrative (Ano), an i-adj (ookii), a positional phrase (ki no shita ni), a na-adj (kirei na), and animate existence (neko ga imasu).

importantRules.ruleText: 'Kirei' (beautiful/clean) and 'Yuumei' (famous) end in 'i', but they are strictly na-adjectives. Do not conjugate them like i-adjectives.
To make an i-adjective negative, you MUST drop the final 'i'.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Takai -> Taka -> Takaku arimasen. (NOT Takaiku arimasen).

importantRules.ruleText: Plants and trees are alive, but they do not move on their own. Therefore, you use 'Arimasu' for trees, not 'Imasu'.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Ki ga arimasu. (There is a tree).

cultureContext.contextText: Japanese ghosts (Yurei) and monsters (Yokai) are considered animate, so you use 'Imasu' when talking about them. There is a famous urban legend about 'Toshokan no Hanako-san' (Hanako of the Toilet). If you ask 'Hanako-san ga imasu ka?' (Is Hanako there?), she might reply!
dosList: Use 'Imasu' for people, animals, insects, and mythical creatures.
dontsList: Do not use 'Imasu' for robots or cars, even if they move on their own. They are inanimate (Arimasu).

summary: Adjectives are split into i-adjectives (drop 'i' add 'ku arimasen' for negative) and na-adjectives (add 'na' before nouns). 'Arimasu' is for inanimate existence, 'Imasu' is for animate existence. Combine these with positional nouns (ue, naka, shita) to describe your environment in deep detail.

checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: たかくありません (Takaku arimasen)

checkpointQuestions.questionText: Which verb do you use to say 'There is a dog'?
Translate: 'On top of the desk' (desk = tsukue, top = ue).
checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: つくえの うえに (Tsukue no ue ni)

keyTakeaways: i-adjective negative: drop i, add ku arimasen.
na-adjective modifier: add na before noun.
Location formula: Noun no Position ni.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: High-density block introducing adjective conjugations and existence/location grammar.
importantFacts: Kirei and Yuumei are na-adjective exceptions.
The particle 'ga' marks the subject of existence.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learner must process long sentences identifying location, existence, and descriptive qualities simultaneously.

feedbackRules.onPass: Incredible job processing this massive lesson! You are ready to describe the world.
feedbackRules.onFail: Take it slow. Focus first on separating Arimasu vs Imasu. Then memorize the position words.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good start. Review the negative form of i-adjectives (dropping the 'i') as this is a very common sticking point.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: おいしい。しずか。たかいです。たかくありません。しずかです。しずかじゃありません。おいしい すし。きれいな ひと。くるまが あります。いぬが います。つくえの うえに ほんが あります。きの したに いぬが いません。

commonMistakes: Saying 'Kirei na desu'. (You only use 'na' when a noun follows immediately after).
